name: Button has non-empty accessible name
description: This rule checks that each `button` element has a non-empty accessible name.
applicability_text: This rule applies to elements that are [included in the accessibility tree][] and have a [semantic role](#semantic-role) of `button`, except for `input` elements with a `type` [attribute value] of `image`.
expectations: Each target element has an [accessible name][] that is not empty (`""`).

examples.name: Passed Example 1
examples.description: This `button` element has an [accessible name][] because of its text content.

examples.name: Passed Example 2
examples.description: This `input` element has an [accessible name][] because of its `value` attribute.

examples.name: Passed Example 3
examples.description: This `button` element has an [accessible name][] because of its `aria-label` attribute.

examples.name: Passed Example 4
examples.description: This element with a `button` role has an [accessible name][] because of its `aria-label` attribute.

examples.name: Passed Example 5
examples.description: This `button` element with the `disabled` attribute has an [accessible name][] because of its text content.

examples.name: Passed Example 6
examples.description: This off screen `button` element has an [accessible name][] because of its text content.

examples.name: Passed Example 7
examples.description: This `input` element has an [accessible name][] because of the default accessible name for an `input` element with a `type` attribute set to `reset`.

examples.name: Failed Example 1
examples.description: This `button` element has no [accessible name][] because it has no content or attribute that can provide it.

examples.name: Failed Example 2
examples.description: This `button` element has no [accessible name][]. The `value` attribute does not provide an [accessible name][] for `button` elements, only when an `input` element's [state of the `type` attribute](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/input.html#states-of-the-type-attribute) is `button`, `submit` or `reset`.

examples.name: Failed Example 3
examples.description: This element with the `button` role has no [accessible name][] because it has no content or attribute that can provide it.

examples.name: Failed Example 4
examples.description: This off screen `button` element has no [accessible name][] because it has no content or attribute that can provide it.
examples.code:
<html>
	<style>
		.notInPage {
			position: absolute;
			left: -9999px;
			top: -9999px;
		}
	</style>
	<body>
		<button class="notInPage" value="delete"></button>
	</body>
</html>

examples.name: Failed Example 5
examples.description: This `button` element has an [explicit role][] of `none`. However, it is [focusable][] (by default). Thus it has a [semantic role][] of `button` due to [Presentational Roles Conflict Resolution][]. It has an empty [accessible name][].

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 1
examples.description: This `input` element has a `type` attribute set to `image`. These images are tested in a separate rule which also tests [success criterion 1.1.1 Non-text Content](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#non-text-content).

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 2
examples.description: This `button` element does not need an [accessible name][] because it is not included in the accessibility tree.

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 3
examples.description: This `button` element has a `link` role. Links are tested in a separate rule which also tests [success criterion 2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context)](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#link-purpose-in-context).

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 4
examples.description: There is no element with a semantic role of `button`.

examples.name: Inapplicable Example 5
examples.description: This `button` element has an [explicit role][] of `none`; it is not [focusable][] because it is `disabled`. Thus it has a [semantic role][] of `none`.

background: This rule considers an exception for "image buttons" (i.e., `input` elements with a `type` [attribute value] of `image`). Image buttons failing this rule would fail [Success Criterion 4.1.2](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/name-role-value) and [Success Criterion 1.1.1](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/Understanding/non-text-content) which is not part of the accessibility requirements for this rule.
assumptions:
- The rule assumes that all buttons are [user interface components as defined by WCAG 2](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/#dfn-user-interface-components).

- Some elements have a role of `button` and a default accessible name defined by the [HTML Accessibility API Mapping][html aam input button], for example `input` elements whose `type` [attribute value][] is either `submit` or `reset`. This rule considers that these default names can be descriptive and therefore does not fail them.
accessibility_support: - Implementation of [Presentational Roles Conflict Resolution][] varies from one browser or assistive technology to another. Depending on this, some elements can have a [semantic role][] of `button` and fail this rule with some technology but users of other technologies would not experience any accessibility issue.
